title: Topic 495: Why Battle Royales Conquered the World: Last One Standing Appeal
content:
> **Topic 495: Why Battle Royales Conquered the World: Last One Standing Appeal**

**Category:** Gaming Culture and Psychology

### ABSTRACT

In an era where humanity has solved most of its existential problems, a new one has emerged: what to do with our weekends. The answer, it seems, is to voluntarily parachute onto a remote island with 99 other individuals, armed with nothing but a pickaxe and a prayer, to engage in a brutal fight to the death. This paper delves into the psychological underpinnings of the Battle Royale phenomenon, a genre of video games that has taken the world by storm. We posit that this global obsession is not a sign of impending societal collapse, but rather a testament to the masterful manipulation of fundamental human desires. Through a rigorous, albeit slightly satirical, analysis of Self-Determination Theory, the concept of "flow," and the intoxicating allure of risk, we will demonstrate how Battle Royale games have engineered the perfect psychological cocktail to keep players coming back for more. This paper will explore the intricate dance between autonomy, competence, and relatedness that unfolds in these virtual arenas, and what it reveals about our collective psyche. In short, we will explain why millions of people find immense joy in being the last one standing, even if it's just in their pajamas.

### INTRODUCTION

The digital age has presented us with a plethora of new and exciting ways to spend our leisure time. We can connect with friends across the globe, learn new skills with the click of a button, or watch an endless stream of cat videos. Yet, a significant portion of the global population has chosen to spend their free time in a state of perpetual, adrenaline-fueled anxiety. They are the players of Battle Royale games, and their numbers are legion. From the gritty, realistic battlegrounds of *PlayerUnknown's Battlegrounds (PUBG)* to the cartoonish, vibrant world of *Fortnite*, the formula is deceptively simple: a large number of players are dropped onto a massive, ever-shrinking map, and the last one standing wins. It is a digital reimagining of *The Hunger Games*, but with more dancing and fewer dystopian political undertones.

But what is it about this seemingly brutal and repetitive formula that has captivated the hearts and minds of millions? Why have these games become a cultural phenomenon, spawning a new generation of celebrity streamers and permeating mainstream culture to an unprecedented degree? This paper argues that the success of the Battle Royale genre is not a fluke, but a carefully constructed psychological experience that taps into our most basic human needs. By examining the genre through the lens of Self-Determination Theory (SDT), we will explore how these games provide a powerful sense of autonomy, competence, and relatedness. We will also delve into the concept of "flow," the state of complete immersion that these games so expertly induce, and the intoxicating psychological dance of risk and reward that keeps players on the edge of their seats. This paper will take you on a journey through the academic and the absurd, from the hallowed halls of psychological research to the chaotic, meme-filled trenches of the Battle Royale battlefield. We will analyze the rise of the streamer, the power of the battle pass, and what the global obsession with being the last one standing says about us as a species. So, grab your virtual parachute and your favorite in-game emote; it's time to drop in.

### LITERATURE REVIEW

To understand the magnetic pull of Battle Royale games, one cannot simply observe the chaos from afar; one must venture into the dense, often contradictory, jungle of academic literature. For decades, scholars have been fascinated by the psychology of video games, and their findings provide a crucial foundation for our analysis. While some early studies focused on the potential negative impacts of gaming, a more nuanced understanding has emerged, one that recognizes the profound psychological benefits that games can offer. It is within this body of work that we find the keys to unlocking the Battle Royale phenomenon.

At the heart of our investigation lies Self-Determination Theory (SDT), a broad framework of human motivation developed by Edward L. Deci and Richard M. Ryan [1]. SDT posits that all human beings have three innate psychological needs: **autonomy**, the need to feel in control of one's own actions and decisions; **competence**, the need to feel effective and capable; and **relatedness**, the need to feel connected to others. When these needs are satisfied, individuals are more likely to be motivated, engaged, and experience a sense of well-being. A growing body of research has applied SDT to the context of video games, with compelling results. Studies have consistently shown that games that satisfy these three needs are more enjoyable and lead to greater player engagement [2]. As Fernandez de Henestrosa, Billieux, and Melzer argue in their seminal work, "Last Man Standing: Battle Royale Games Through the Lens of Self-Determination Theory," the Battle Royale genre is a masterclass in satisfying these fundamental needs [3].

Beyond the foundational principles of SDT, we must also consider the concept of "flow," a term coined by psychologist Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi [4]. Flow is the state of being "in the zone," a state of complete absorption in an activity where time seems to melt away. It is the delicate balance between the challenge of a task and the skill of the individual. If the challenge is too great, the player becomes anxious; if it is too easy, they become bored. Battle Royale games, with their dynamic and unpredictable nature, are exceptionally adept at inducing a state of flow. The ever-present threat of elimination, combined with the constant need to make strategic decisions, keeps players in a state of heightened focus and engagement. As one Reddit user eloquently put it, "Flow is that magical feeling when time fades away and you're able to perform at an optimal state of consciousness" [5].

Finally, no discussion of Battle Royale games would be complete without acknowledging the intoxicating allure of risk and reward. The entire genre is built upon a foundation of high-stakes decision-making. Every choice, from where to land to when to engage in combat, carries with it a potential for glorious victory or swift defeat. This constant risk-reward calculation triggers a release of dopamine in the brain, the neurotransmitter associated with pleasure and reward [6]. This is the same neurochemical process that makes gambling so addictive, and it is a powerful force in keeping players hooked. The thrill of outsmarting an opponent, of pulling off a risky maneuver, of being the last one standing against all odds, is a potent psychological cocktail that is hard to resist.

### ANALYSIS: THE PSYCHOLOGICAL COCKTAIL OF BATTLE ROYALES

Having waded through the theoretical underpinnings, we now arrive at the heart of the matter: how, precisely, do Battle Royale games concoct their irresistible psychological brew? The answer lies not in a single ingredient, but in the masterful blending of several key elements, each designed to target and satisfy our deepest-seated psychological needs. It is a symphony of controlled chaos, a masterwork of motivational design that leaves players simultaneously exhilarated, frustrated, and utterly captivated.

First and foremost is the profound sense of **autonomy**. Unlike many linear video games that guide players along a predetermined path, Battle Royale games throw open the doors to a vast, open world and say, "Go nuts." From the very first moment, the player is in control. Where do you land? Do you drop into a high-traffic, high-loot area, risking immediate confrontation for the chance at better gear? Or do you opt for a quieter, more remote location, sacrificing early-game riches for a greater chance of survival? Every decision, from the choice of weapon to the path taken across the map, is the player's own. This is not the illusion of choice; it is genuine, unadulterated freedom. This radical autonomy is a powerful motivator, giving players a sense of ownership over their own destiny in a world that often feels anything but.

Next, we have the intoxicating allure of **competence**. Battle Royale games are, at their core, a relentless pursuit of mastery. The learning curve is steep, and the initial experience for most new players is a series of swift, brutal, and often comical eliminations. Yet, with each match, players learn a little more. They discover the nuances of the weapons, the strategic advantages of the terrain, and the subtle art of the ambush. The game provides a constant stream of feedback, both positive and negative, that allows for a tangible sense of progression. The first kill, the first time reaching the top ten, and the ultimate triumph of being the last one standing—the coveted "Winner Winner, Chicken Dinner"—are powerful affirmations of competence. This journey from bumbling novice to seasoned veteran is a deeply satisfying one, and it is a key driver of long-term engagement.

Finally, we have the crucial element of **relatedness**. While the "last one standing" objective may seem to promote a lone-wolf mentality, the reality is that Battle Royale games are deeply social experiences. The introduction of squad-based modes has transformed the genre from a solitary struggle into a cooperative endeavor. The shared experience of a match—the frantic calls for backup, the heroic revives, the coordinated assaults on enemy positions—forges strong bonds between players. Even the shared misery of a crushing defeat can be a source of camaraderie. Furthermore, the rise of streaming platforms has created a global community of players and spectators, all united by their shared passion for the game. This sense of belonging, of being part of something larger than oneself, is a powerful psychological motivator that should not be underestimated.

### REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES: TALES FROM THE BATTLEGROUND

The theoretical frameworks and psychological principles we've discussed are not mere abstractions; they are vividly illustrated in the real-world phenomena that have sprung up around the Battle Royale genre. The stories, personalities, and cultural artifacts that have emerged from these virtual battlegrounds offer a rich tapestry of evidence for the genre's profound psychological impact.

One cannot discuss the Battle Royale phenomenon without paying homage to its two undisputed kings: *PlayerUnknown's Battlegrounds (PUBG)* and *Fortnite*. *PUBG*, with its gritty, realistic aesthetic and unforgiving gameplay, was the game that first catapulted the genre into the mainstream. It was a raw, visceral experience that appealed to players seeking a hardcore, military-simulation-style challenge. *Fortnite*, on the other hand, took a decidedly different approach. With its vibrant, cartoonish graphics, whimsical tone, and unique building mechanic, it lowered the barrier to entry and broadened the genre's appeal to a much wider audience. The rivalry between these two titans is a fascinating case study in market segmentation and the power of brand identity. *PUBG* catered to the purists, the players who craved a sense of realism and high-stakes tension. *Fortnite*, in contrast, captured the hearts and minds of a younger demographic, with its accessible gameplay, constant updates, and a deluge of crossover content that transformed the game into a veritable cultural melting pot.

The rise of the Battle Royale genre has also given birth to a new kind of celebrity: the professional streamer. Individuals like Tyler "Ninja" Blevins and Michael "Shroud" Grzesiek have become household names, amassing millions of followers and earning fortunes by broadcasting their gameplay to a global audience. These streamers are more than just skilled players; they are entertainers, community leaders, and, in a sense, modern-day gladiators. They embody the aspirational aspect of the Battle Royale experience, showcasing a level of skill and strategic thinking that most players can only dream of. Their success is a testament to the spectator appeal of the genre; watching a top-tier player navigate the treacherous landscape of a Battle Royale match is a thrilling experience in its own right.

Finally, the cultural impact of Battle Royale games can be seen in the countless memes, inside jokes, and viral moments that have permeated the internet and popular culture. From the ubiquitous "floss" dance from *Fortnite* to the desperate cry of "I need a weapon!" in *Apex Legends*, these games have created a shared language and a set of cultural touchstones for a generation of players. These humorous and relatable moments, born from the shared struggles and triumphs of the game, are a powerful force for community building. They are the digital equivalent of war stories, tales of epic victories and hilarious defeats that are passed down from player to player, strengthening the bonds of the community and reinforcing the game's cultural significance.

### DISCUSSION: WHAT IT ALL MEANS

The meteoric rise of the Battle Royale genre is more than just a passing fad; it is a significant cultural and psychological phenomenon with far-reaching implications. The insights we can glean from the success of these games extend beyond the realm of entertainment and offer valuable lessons for understanding human behavior and consumer habits in the digital age.

The business model of many Battle Royale games, particularly the "battle pass" system, is a masterclass in gamified engagement. By offering a steady stream of cosmetic rewards for completing in-game challenges, developers have created a powerful incentive for players to return to the game day after day. This model, which leverages the psychological principles of goal-setting and variable rewards, has proven to be incredibly effective at retaining players and generating revenue. The success of the battle pass has not gone unnoticed, and we are already seeing its influence in other areas of the software and entertainment industries. The gamification of everyday life is a trend that is likely to continue, and the lessons learned from the Battle Royale genre will undoubtedly play a significant role in shaping its future.

The success of Battle Royale games also raises interesting questions about the future of entertainment. The line between player and spectator is becoming increasingly blurred, with millions of people tuning in to watch their favorite streamers compete. This has created a new form of interactive entertainment, one that is more engaging and participatory than traditional media. As technology continues to evolve, we can expect to see even more innovative and immersive entertainment experiences that build upon the foundation laid by the Battle Royale genre. The day when we can all participate in a real-life, non-lethal version of a Battle Royale may not be as far off as we think.

Finally, the global obsession with being the last one standing invites us to reflect on our own society. In a world that is increasingly interconnected and collaborative, the appeal of a game that celebrates individual survival is a curious paradox. Perhaps it is a primal instinct, a remnant of our evolutionary past when the ability to outlast our competitors was a matter of life and death. Or perhaps it is a form of escapism, a safe and consequence-free way to experience the thrill of competition and the satisfaction of victory. Whatever the reason, the enduring popularity of the Battle Royale genre suggests that the desire to be the last one standing is a powerful and deeply ingrained part of the human psyche.

### CONCLUSION

In the grand, chaotic theatre of the digital age, the Battle Royale genre has emerged as a blockbuster production. It is a masterful blend of psychological principles, a carefully crafted experience that caters to our most fundamental human desires. Through the lens of Self-Determination Theory, we have seen how these games provide a powerful sense of autonomy, competence, and relatedness. We have explored the intoxicating allure of the "flow" state and the addictive nature of risk and reward. We have witnessed the rise of a new generation of celebrity streamers and the birth of a global community united by a shared passion for the game.

The success of the Battle Royale genre is not a mystery; it is a testament to the power of good game design. It is a reminder that, in a world of endless distractions, the most compelling experiences are those that challenge us, empower us, and connect us to one another. So, the next time you find yourself parachuting onto a virtual island, remember that you are not just playing a game; you are participating in a global psychological experiment. And as you fight for your virtual life, take a moment to appreciate the beautiful, chaotic, and utterly human spectacle of it all. After all, in the end, there can be only one.
references:
[1] Ryan, R. M., & Deci, E. L. (2000). Self-determination theory and the facilitation of intrinsic motivation, social development, and well-being. *American Psychologist, 55*(1), 68–78.

[2] Ryan, R. M., Rigby, C. S., & Przybylski, A. K. (2006). The motivational pull of video games: A self-determination theory approach. *Motivation and Emotion, 30*(4), 344–360.

[3] Fernandez de Henestrosa, M., Billieux, J., & Melzer, A. (2022). Last man standing: Battle royale games through the lens of self-determination theory. *Games and Culture, 18*(4), 427–448.

[4] Csikszentmihalyi, M. (1990). *Flow: The psychology of optimal experience*. Harper & Row.

[5] u/TheChadHimself. (2020, August 5). *How to get in "The Zone" (Flow State) while Gaming* [Video]. Reddit. https://www.reddit.com/r/CruciblePlaybook/comments/i4byeb/how_to_get_in_the_zone_flow_state_while_gaming/

[6] Zald, D. H., Boileau, I., El-Dearedy, W., Gunn, R., McGlone, F., Dichter, G. S., & Dagher, A. (2004). Dopamine transmission in the human striatum during monetary reward tasks. *Journal of Neuroscience, 24*(17), 4105–4112.
